EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring breakup of the river ice on the Kuskokwim River resulted in several ice jams along the river that impacted multiple communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the spring river ice breakup front moved downstream on the Kuskokwim River, an ice jam formed downstream of the villages of Upper Kalskag and Lower Kalskag. The road connecting the two villages was flooded and impassable. Access to the shared power plant was also cut off. Many yards had floodwaters affecting them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring breakup of the river ice on the Kuskokwim River resulted in several ice jams along the river that impacted multiple communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the spring river ice breakup front moved downstream on the Kuskokwim River, an ice jam formed downstream of the village of Tuluksak. Reports were received of water levels rising rapidly into the lower portions of the village, and the community prepared for more substantial evacuations. Thankfully, the water levels began to slowly fall as the ice jam released slowly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring breakup of the river ice on the Kuskokwim River resulted in several ice jams along the river that impacted multiple communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the spring river ice breakup front moved downstream on the Kuskokwim River, an ice jam formed near Birch Crossing, downstream of Aniak. Water levels rose steadily in Aniak and began to flood the low-lying areas of town with up to 6 ft depths of water. The most significantly impacted areas were the gravel pit outside of town, and the barge landing downstream of the main town. Eventually the ice jam released and water levels steadily dropped.

CZ_NAME: INNER CHANNELS FROM KUPREANOF ISLAND TO ETOLIN ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather front swept over the central panhandle on 24 February, spreading snow over the cooler air trapped near the surface. An easterly flow pattern ahead of the front/low resulted in some upslope flow to the easterly slopes increasing the snowfall.  Temperatures began warming to in the afternoon leading to a wetter snowfall in the afternoon and compaction of the snowpack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Petersburg trained spotter measured 10 inches of new snow at 1525 2/24. Impact was snow removal. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another outflow event was characterized by a 1050 MB high over the Yukon and temperatures in the mid -40s over  Canada.  Strong pressure gradients and moderate cold advection caused high wind gusts at the Skagway marine exchange site.  A more glancing blow of cold air as opposed to the last system combined with the lack of strong low pressure to the south limited gusts below 70 mph and also the length of the outflow event. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous gusts 60 to 65 mph were observed at the Skagway Airport during the night of 2/7 and all day on 2/8. No damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 979 MB gale force low moved onto Vancouver Island on the afternoon of 2/28 with 1035 MB High over the Yukon. This was a setup for a Taku wind event for Downtown Juneau and Douglas. The peak gust was 92 mph from a marine Exchange observation Downtown. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Taku wind gusts for Downtown and Douglas ranged from 65 to 92 mph during the night of 2/28 and daytime on 3/1. No damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Taku wind affecting Downtown Juneau and Douglas occurred on 3/22 as a result of 1037 MB high pressure over the Yukon and a 993 MB low over the Hecate Strait. ||Strong gusty winds  with several gusts above 60 kt from early morning into the afternoon hours.  Bursts of winds from downbursts and lighter gusts to 50 mph through the day. Occasional peaks to 60mph into the evening after the warning ended.  Lenticular clouds in evidence for the sky cover.  Northern inside channels had peak gusts of 60-90mph. Tenakee Springs had occasional gusts hitting high wind criteria and waterspout observed by the mayor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This Taku wind caused some shingle damage in West Juneau on 3/22 with fence damage, roof of chicken coop blown off, and back door damage reported at Douglas residence.	Douglas Boat harbor  84 mph (twitter post), Peak gust of 91 mph at 944 AKST.  Lots of gusts in the 70s mph.  Power outage Downtown Juneau at 0245 AKST.  Tenakee Springs Mayor observed a waterspout on 3/22.

CZ_NAME: CAPE FAIRWEATHER TO CAPE SUCKLING COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low tracked ESE across the northern gulf into the eastern gulf.  Warm moist air  overrunning cold air at the surface caused measurable snow to much of the northern and central Panhandle. Amounts were highly variable. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow measured at the Yakutat Airport and Downtown was 7.0 inches in 12 hours. Impact was snow removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western portion of Alaska combined with a low over the Panhandle to cause a tight gradient over Southcentral. A front passing through the Chugach mountains increased the gap flow to warning level through northerly gaps and into Valdez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds peaked through Thompson Pass at 7:20 a.m. at 83 mph with 102 mph gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western portion of Alaska combined with a low over the Panhandle to cause a tight gradient over Southcentral. A front passing through the Chugach mountains increased the gap flow to warning level through northerly gaps and into Valdez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valdez ASOS peaked later in the evening, beginning about 7 p.m. The peak at the airport was 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska combined with a high pressure system over the mainland to cause a tight gradient through the Chugach Range. This was further enhanced by a cold dome of air located over the Copper River Basin, bringing high winds to Thompson Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road Weather Information System station in Thompson Pass (Elev 2740 ft) reported gust of 80 mph starting at 2:50pm 1/28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska formed a new center close to the Prince William Sound. As this low brought a trough through the Prince William Sound area, the trough combined with a dome of cold air over the Copper River Basin to cause blizzard conditions through the gaps of the Chugach Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from DOT Maintenance Camp in Thompson Pass indicated variable visibility between 0-100 ft. Worst conditions between miles 25-28 on the Richardson Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska shifted westward towards Kodiak Island, tightening the pressure gradient. A series of troughs increased the winds over the Alaska Peninsula and caused near blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: King Cove AWOS reported a peak gust of 67 knots at 5:23 p.m. on January 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong arctic high pressure had built into the Yukon Territory up to 1037 MB on 1/9 and 1/10. Very strong outflow winds were reported in Downtown Haines up to 60 mph causing damage on the afternoon of 1/9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marine Exchange observation recorded gusts to 60 MPH for the Haines Harbor (Downtown) on the afternoon of 1/9. Local newspaper reported that a ski rack was torn off a car and went through the window of a downtown business. Other damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front brought precipitation into the Panhandle on 1/13. Temperatures started at the surface well below freezing along the Klondike Highway.  Strong warm air advection was occurring as the front passed which produced periods of heavy snow along the Klondike Highway during the night of 1/13. At least 7 inches of new snow fall along the highway. Precipitation rates diminished while temperatures continued to warm early on 1/14 which ended the warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 7 inches of new snow fell along the Klondike Highway during the night of 1/13. No damage reported. Impact was snow removal by DOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the first day of February, a strong area of frontogenesis developed with the front moving north into the southern panhandle. N-NE-E outflow wind will kept temperatures cold enough on the north side of the front to allow snow to get up to southern Admiralty Island. Increases in 850mb temps above freezing late Thursday night into Friday morning made the rain/snow line critical and difficult to forecast. Significant snowfall occurred for  Port Alexander, Petersburg, Wrangell, Point Baker and Coffman Cove. Ketchikan had wintry mix. No damage was reports and the impact was snow removal.||Some places could have had blizzard conditions with this event due to strong outflow. Port Alex had gusts of around 40kt during bands of snow. Highest snow falls extended a little farther south then expected. Petersburg, to Whale Pass to Thorne Bay, and Coffman Cove got buried. lighter amounts elsewhere. Snow changed to rain early in Ketchikan and overnight at Kasaan limited snow amounts to 1 to 2 inches there. Hyder still snowing as of 11 am 2/2 with 7 inches accumulated so far, and up to  9.3 inches as of 730 pm. Unknown about Port Alexander and Kake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Happy Ground Hog's Day. Petersburg, Kake, and Point Baker got at least 7 inches of snow in 12 hours overnight. 6 inches at Wrangell. Impact was snow removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front stalled out over Icy Strait on 3/26. This caused overrunning and convection aloft due to steeper lapse rates in snow growth zone. The Pelican / Elfin Cove Area and Juneau got 6 to 10 inches storm total. Impact was snow removal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Juneau Airport measured 9.2 inches and the WFO measured 8.6 inches storm total. Other areas around town got 6 to 8 inches of new snow. Impact was snow removal but the snow melted off very quickly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the Gulf kept snow showers over the Haines Borough longer than originally anticipated. Snow showers were heavy at times for 5 to 7 inches over the area. Impact was snow removal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Majority of the snow fell during the daylight hours with 5 inches measured Downtown by 1900 AKST and was falling with heavy rates at the time. Snowfall rate became light  around 2000 then ended by 2300. State of snow was heavy and definitely snowman making material.  Storm total was around 7 inches up the highway toward Customs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moving in from the west and low pressure moving in from the east combined to tighten the gradient over the eastern Chugach mountains. A strong shortwave moving in from the north enhanced the winds through channeled terrain, taking them to warning level. This caused some wind damage at Tsaina Lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thompson Pass RWIS station recorded 89 mph peak gust. Visibility through Thompson Pass was reduced to near blizzard conditions due to blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the Middle Tanana Valley during the overnight hours of the 17th. Precipitation began as freezing rain due to enough warm air in place aloft when the precipitation started. Light freezing rain occurred over several hours and there were multiple car accidents reported due to icy roads. Less then one tenth of an inch of ice reported. The precipitation changed to snow late in the evening and heavy wet snow fell overnight.  There was a sharp gradient of snowflall amounts. 7.4 inches fell at the Fairbanks Airport and only a trace of snow southeast of Fairbanks. A record precipitation amount of 0.55 inches was set at the Fairbanks Airport on the 17th. This breaks the old record of 0.33 inches set in 2000. A record snowfall of 6.1 inches was also reported at the Fairbanks Airport which fell after midnight. This breaks the old record of 4 inches set in 2008.||Snowfall amounts around the Fairbanks area.||College Hills......7.8 inches.|Fairbanks Airport...7.4 inches.|UAF West Ridge....6.7 inches.|North Pole....Trace.

Cooler air inland along the Haines Highway with continued light northwest surface winds  kept the temperatures from warming during warm moist air overrunning.  Storm total snow 16.0 inches at Haines Customs. Impact was snow removal by DOT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow 16.0 inches at Haines Customs ending at 1200 on 3/11. Impact was snow removal by DOT.

A low pressure system in the Aleutians brought an occluded front through the Alaska Peninsula and Bristol Bay. This combined with cold air over the mainland from a previous high pressure system caused heavy snow to fall in the Bristol Bay region. The approaching front also increased the gradient, causing winds to whip snow through the air, reducing visibility to less than one mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: King Salmon Weather Service Office observed 10 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved up the east side of the Kenai Peninsula and into the Prince William Sound. This produced heavy snow across the eastern Kenai Peninsula. Strong winds enhanced by channeled terrain through Portage Valley caused near blizzard conditions in that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The snow stake at mile post 69.9 recorded 24 of snow in 24 hours. A school bus went off the road in Moose Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valdez ASOS reported 72 knots peak wind in the undecoded observation. This was observed by personnel in the tower.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hatcher Pass Avalanche Center reported an avalanche in Hatcher Pass closed the road to the ski area at the top. 10 people were stranded at the ski area for 24 hours while DOT cleared the road. No one was injured.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front lifted north with a few short-waves moving across the coast mountains on Thursday 4/5. Cross-barrier flow was minimal at the critical level, but warming aloft will created an inversion at the ridgetop along Gastineau Channel, and cross-barrier gradient at the surface was quite strong.  All three ingredients caused a Taku wind event for Downtown Juneau and Douglas on 4/5 into 4/6. Damage was minor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Juneau AML dock observed gusts to 61 mph at 1322 on 4/5. The Juneau AJ Dock observed gusts to 83 mph at 1102 on 4/5 during this Taku wind event. Damage was limited to Lawn furniture displaced and garbage cans blown over. Tree branches were broken on Douglas at 0930 AKST. An old Christmas tree was blown off a porch. No monetary value was assigned to this damage.

CZ_NAME: DIXON ENTRANCE TO CAPE DECISION COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex low pressure presisted over the entire Gulf on 4/9 & 4/10. A very strong wave developed south of Haida Gwaii and skirted the coast of SE Alaska causing storm force wind and hurricane force gusts. There was some damage reported, and the peak wind was 100 MPH. The strong wave moved off the coast and into the western Gulf on the morning of 4/11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Klawock Airport had a measured gust 58 mph.  Forecasters at the Juneau Office cannot recall seeing that so high. Hydaburg AWOS was out of service. Cape Decision had several measured gusts around 74 mph.  No damage has been reported so far.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 970 mb low pressure system moved through the Bering Sea, bringing a weather front across southcentral. Strong southerly flow behind the front created mountain waves, particularly in the Anchorage Bowl, where 70 mph winds reached into the center portion of the city. There was much damage to private property and power outages across the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Public weather station on Spendlove Drive recorded a gust to 103 mph. This was the highest gust during the event. Regular gusts up to 70 mph were observed in midtown, with gusts between 80 and 90 mph along the Anchorage Hillside. Up to 27,500 customers were without power at one time during the event. A home along the Anchorage Hillside lost its roof early in the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, anomalously strong upper level trough and associated surface low dug southward across Western Alaska. As a result, nearly the entire atmosphere across Southern Alaska shifted to southwesterly flow, which brought copious amounts of Pacific moisture into Southern Alaska. This rainfall combined with already high water levels due to snowmelt from anomalously warm temperatures earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dike eroded along Sawmill Creek causing flooding on 2 properties in the Clyde King Subdivision to flood; 1 house has 1.5 ft of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, anomalously strong upper level trough and associated surface low dug southward across Western Alaska. As a result, nearly the entire atmosphere across Southern Alaska shifted to southwesterly flow, which brought copious amounts of Pacific moisture into Southern Alaska. This rainfall combined with already high water levels due to snowmelt from anomalously warm temperatures earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yentna and Skwentna rivers, already high due to snowmelt, were expected to reach near bankfull during the second week of July as the weather pattern turned wetter. On July 10th, a local lodge near the confluence of the Yentna River and Lake Creek reported flooding in cabins and outbuildings resulting in 18 inches of water getting inside. The Flood Advisory was issued as a result of this report. Later that same day, an update from Lake Creek was received saying that the river had risen to 2-3 feet above the bank and that most of the property, including numerous water front lodges, were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: First significant freeze of the Fall 2018 season affecting SE Alaska occurred during the early morning hours of September 17th. Clear skies and calm wind caused radiative cooling to drop temperatures either for prolonged below 32F or brief drops to around 28F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PAGS AWOS measured prolonged temperatures below 32F between 0100 and 0800 on the morning of 09/17. Lowest temperature 27F. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low digging southward across Southwest Alaska, brought moist flow off the Gulf into Southcentral on southeasterly winds. This brought higher than normal rainfall to the northern and western Susitna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on the Yentna River at Lake Creek went into minor flood stage for a brief period on August 14th. McDougall's Lodge Cabins were evacuated due to flooding water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure with a long fetch from the North Pacific to the Gulf of Alaska produced a very strong and wet weather system. The atmospheric river slammed into northern southeast Alaska panhandle from August 7th and persisted through the 10th. The area received moderate to heavy rainfall with stations reporting two to four inches of precipitation at sea level with five inches at higher elevations. Along with the heavy rainfall there was very high freezing levels which ranged from eight to ten thousand feet. These high freezing level accompanied with the heavy rain increased the runoff from any snow left in the mountains and ice melt from area glaciers. All of the rain and snow/ice melt produce moderate flooding along the Taiya River and flooded the Chilkoot trail, part of the Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park, near Dyea Alaska. The river crested around seventeen point thirty five feet which is above moderate flood stage of seventeen feet in the evening hours on July 23rd. The Mendenhall Lake also rose above moderate flood stage of ten feet and produced moderate flooding along the Mendenhall Lake area. The Mendenhall Lake gauge crested at ten point forty-two feet  in the afternoon of August 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a very dry period for the northern inner channels of Southeast Alaska moderate to heavy rains moved over the area on August 7th. The high rain rates continued for the next forty-eight to seventy-two hours. Rainfall totals from this time frame ranged from two to three inches near sea level with four to five inches in the headwaters at higher elevations. The very warm temperatures created very high freezing levels that increase runoff from snow or ice melt. The Taiya River started to rise through the early morning hours of August 7th and went above minor flood stage of sixteen point five feet by the late afternoon. The river continued to rise at a stair step fashion through the night and into the morning hours of August 8th. By the early afternoon the water levels rose above moderate flood stage of seventeen feet and then leveled off some as the rain rate relax some through the early evening hours of August 8th. There was a big push of moisture that moved over the area in the early morning hours of August 9th and the river began to rise very steeply. The Taiya River crested in the late morning on August 9th at eighteen point twenty-one feet almost a foot and half over the moderate flood stage of seventeen feet. The rain slowly diminished through the overnight hours of August 10th and the river slowly receded to be below minor flood stage by the morning hours of August 10th. The National Park Service reported that there was significant flooding along the Chilkoot trail with waist deep water along the lower portions of the trail. The international trail was closed for a few days due to the flooding and potential dangers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure with a long fetch from the North Pacific to the Gulf of Alaska produced a very strong and wet weather system. The atmospheric river slammed into northern southeast Alaska panhandle from August 7th and persisted through the 10th. The area received moderate to heavy rainfall with stations reporting two to four inches of precipitation at sea level with five inches at higher elevations. Along with the heavy rainfall there was very high freezing levels which ranged from eight to ten thousand feet. These high freezing level accompanied with the heavy rain increased the runoff from any snow left in the mountains and ice melt from area glaciers. All of the rain and snow/ice melt produce moderate flooding along the Taiya River and flooded the Chilkoot trail, part of the Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park, near Dyea Alaska. The river crested around seventeen point thirty five feet which is above moderate flood stage of seventeen feet in the evening hours on July 23rd. The Mendenhall Lake also rose above moderate flood stage of ten feet and produced moderate flooding along the Mendenhall Lake area. The Mendenhall Lake gauge crested at ten point forty-two feet  in the afternoon of August 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a very dry period for the northern inner channels of Southeast Alaska moderate to heavy rains moved over the area on August 7th. The high rain rates continued for the next forty-eight to seventy-two hours. Rainfall totals from this time frame ranged from two to three inches near sea level with four to five inches at higher elevations. The very warm temperatures created very high freezing levels that increase runoff from snow or ice melt. There was also a release of water from the glacier dammed lake in Suicide Basin that added runoff into the Mendenhall River system, but less water than the first release earlier in the summer. The Mendenhall Lake started to rise slowly through August 7th, by the morning of August 8th the rate of rise on the lake and along the river increased as the heavy rain moved over the area. The Mendenhall Lake went above minor flood stage of nine feet in the afternoon and continued on the steady rise through the overnight hours and went over moderate flood stage of nine feet. The Mendenhall Lake crested at ten point forty-two feet by the afternoon on August 9th. As the heavy rain started to diminish the water levels began to recede and the lake and river went below minor flood stage in the early morning hours of August 10th. There was minor to moderate flooding around Mendenhall Lake and the upper portions of the river above Back Loop Road. The Mendenhall campground was evacuated due flooding of up to two feet at the entrance and within the campground, also the Skaters Cabin Road was flooded. View Drive was flooded with one foot of water and the road cut off. There was more flooding along the dredge lakes trail area and a few other trails around the Mendenhall Glacier Visitor Center run by the USDA Forest Service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thermal trough lying across the Interior portion of the state extended towards Kuskokwim Delta and Bristol Bay. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving southeastward off the Kamchatka Peninsula rapidly intensified over the Bering Sea, reaching a central pressure of 944 mb by the time it crossed the Aleutian Islands. This system had a second, equally strong, low center that developed in its wake, continuing stormy conditions across the Aleutians as the first low moved onshore. The two low pressure systems brought widespread blizzards and high winds to the Aleutians, the Southwest Mainland, and Southcentral Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shemya Air Station reported frequent gusts to 75mph. Satellite data indicated winds offshore were up to 60 knots over the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving west across the Brooks Range resulted in heavy snow and low visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL at Atigun Pass on the Dalton Highway reported an increase of snow on the ground of 7 inches in 18 hrs. It is estimated that higher amounts of snow fell east of the highway. Strong north winds gusting to 38 mph at Atigun Pass and 28 mph at Anaktuvuk Pass along with low visibility in blowing snow accompanied the snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout spring and the first part of the summer Suicide Basin, a side valley that is dammed by the Mendenhall Glacier, filled with water from snow melt and rainfall. In the afternoon hours of July 17th visual reports showed signs of the basin beginning to drain as the glacier dam was starting to fail. The river gauge in the Mendenhall Lake started to show this inflow of water from the basin through the evening hours and by the morning of the 18th the event was well on its way. For the next 2 days the Mendenhall Lake and River rose five in a half feet and crested at 10.92ft in the afternoon of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the early morning hours of July 18th the Mendenhall Lake and River began to slowly rise as the glacier dammed lake in Suicide Basin about 2 miles from the terminus started to drain. Mendenhall Lake and River continue to rise for the next day and a half as the dammed lake emptied. The water levels reach minor flood stage of nine feet by the morning of July 19th and by the mid-afternoon around 5pm Mendenhall Lake crested at ten point ninety two feet. After the lake and river crested the water level receded very quickly and was below minor flood stage just before midnight on the 19th. There was minor to moderate flooding around Mendenhall Lake and the upper portions of the river above Back Loop Road. The Mendenhall campground was evacuated due flooding of up to three feet at the entrance and within the campground with some damage done to the road system. The West Glacier spur road and the area around Skaters Cabin was flooded with three feet water. View Drive was flooded with two feet of water and the road cut off. There was minor flood damage to some homes that border the river along the road. There was also more flooding along the dredge lakes trail area and and few other trails around the Mendenhall Glacier Visitor Center run by the USDA Forest Service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm force low developed and moved into the eastern Gulf on the afternoon and evening of 12/10. The associated front caused high winds for Prince of Wales Island and the Ketchikan areas. The highest wind gusts were measured at Salmon Landing near Ward Cove at 74 mph. Tree blowdowns were reported blocking roads, but no damage was reported to homes or infrastructure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 957 mb low pressure system moving through the Bering Sea brought cold air and northwest flow to the Central Aleutians. This caused warning level wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Adak NWLON reported 5 hours of warning-level wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On October 23rd, 2018, a rapidly intensifying low brought a front onshore in the early afternoon. This increased winds around Prince William Sound above 70 mph, causing some damage in Cordova. In Whittier, due easterly winds combined with an unusually high tide caused significant erosion and damage along the dock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due easterly winds and an unusually high tide combined to bring waves of an estimated 8 to 9 feet directly into Whittier's harbor. This caused an extensive amount of damage, which was reported by the harbormaster and public works director. Winds during this time reached between 60 and 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between October 12th and October 16th, 2018, an unusually wet weather pattern set up across Seward and the Kenai Peninsula, leading to flooding and erosion in the Seward area, with high water occurring on rivers elsewhere on the Peninsula. This pattern started out with a storm approaching from the south on October 12th. Although it was not a strong system, it did have a weather front extending almost directly southward towards the tropics ��� a pattern that���s known in Alaska as the ���Pineapple Express���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two heavy waves of precipitation between October 12th and 16th each dropped roughly 6��� of rain on the precipitation gage at the headwaters of the Resurrection River, and 6.86��� of precipitation at the Seward airport during the second event. The Resurrection River reached moderate flood stage on Oct 13th and again on the 16th.  Numerous creeks in the Seward area went overbank; closing and washing out roads to critical infrastructure.  The airport runway flooded, and there were mud and rock slides throughout the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate low pressure center stalled in the western Gulf, but southwest flow aloft combined with a strong front caused 6-8 inches of new snow in a short period on 11/8. CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short fused-short lived, strong surface low pressure system developed on a front along the outer coast during the late morning hours on 11/13. This system was unique in that it was poorly handled by numerical models and NOT on any surface analyses. This low cause brief strong wind gusts 60 to 70 mph for Northern Prince of Wales, Kuiu, and southern Baranof Islands. There were also hurricane force gusts at Five Finger Light in Stephens Passage. Some minor damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Port Alexander measured gusts to 63 mph. No damage reported.
